1.matchText: the same way the Khmer on the mainland were doing it
1.blurb: Java and the Khmer are the two great cases of voluntary Indianization in Southeast Asia, by the same mechanism. Neither was ever conquered by India; both bought a Sanskrit-Hindu-Buddhist grammar of kingship through traders and prestige and made it local. Khmer Chapter 1 is the mainland half of the identical process Majapahit's Java went through.

1.matchText: Vietnam absorbed China's civilization as a conquered province
1.blurb: The instructive contrast. Vietnam absorbed a great civilization (China's) as a conquered province resisting its occupier for a thousand years; Java absorbed a great civilization (India's) voluntarily, with no foreign army ever present. Same outcome — a sophisticated Indianized/Sinicized state — opposite causes. Đại Việt Chapter 1 is the conquered case.

Borobudur (c. 825) predates the Khmer's Angkor Wat (begun c. 1113) by nearly three centuries, and both are built on the same imported Indian theory: the temple as an artificial cosmic mountain and the king as its human pivot. Khmer Chapter 5 is the mainland's grandest version of the idea Java built first.

1.matchText: the Maya cities of the Yucatán were sliding into their own collapse
1.blurb: A temporal anchor across the world: while Java was raising Borobudur around 825, the Classic Maya cities of the Yucatán were entering the collapse that ended their own god-king civilization — a useful reminder that the ninth century looked very different on opposite sides of the planet. Maya Chapter 7 is that collapse.

1.matchText: sitting astride the sea lane between China and India
1.blurb: Srivijaya is Majapahit's predecessor in the maritime-power lineage and its tightest comparison: a trade empire with thin soil that got rich taxing the sea lane, where Majapahit would be the kingdom that combined the sea with Java's rice. Srivijaya Chapter 4 works out the same 'what does empire even mean for a thalassocracy' problem from the other island.

Kertanagara's Pamalayu expedition, seen from Sumatra, is the moment Java takes over the strait from a collapsing Srivijaya. Srivijaya Chapter 7 narrates that decline and the same Mongol fleet's attack on Java from the Srivijaya side.
Majapahit inherited the maritime-empire role Srivijaya had pioneered — the Java-based successor to the Sumatra-based thalassocracy.
Java's Indianized kingdoms grew from the same Sanskrit-Hindu-Buddhist seedbed as the Khmer — mainland and island branches of one cultural world.

2.matchText: twice, in 1274 and 1281, he had sent invasion fleets against Japan
2.blurb: The exact parallel. The same Kublai Khan, the same demand for submission, the same overseas armada, the same humiliating retreat — in Japan the weapon was a typhoon the Japanese called the divine wind; in Java it was a man. Yuan Chapter 3 is the failed Japan invasions; Java is the third entry in the same ledger.

Kublai Khan's Yuan dynasty had just completed the conquest of Song China when it turned its fleets outward against Japan, Đại Việt, and Java. Yuan Chapter 1 is the conquest that produced the empire whose punitive expedition Raden Wijaya hijacked.

2.matchText: what Đại Việt would do to a third with stakes in a tidal river
2.blurb: Three failed Mongol seaborne invasions, three different weapons: Japan's typhoon, Đại Việt's tidal-stake river trap, and Java's double-cross. Đại Việt Chapter 5 is the Vietnamese case — the only one, like Java's, defeated by a ruler's nerve rather than by weather.
Majapahit was literally born from a Mongol invasion — Raden Wijaya turned Kublai's Java expedition against itself, like Japan and Dai Viet repelling the Yuan.

6.matchText: Vietnam kept Buddhism and Confucianism as separate, competing state philosophies it never fused
6.blurb: A comparison of state religious strategy. Vietnam kept Buddhism and Confucianism as rival state philosophies and never fused them — the Buddhist Lý state of Đại Việt Chapter 3 later gave way to a Confucian one. Majapahit's Shiva-Buddha is the opposite move: two world religions deliberately welded into one state cult with its own scripture.

6.matchText: the Mughal emperor Akbar would later attempt a synthetic court religion
6.blurb: Akbar's Din-i-Ilahi (Mughal Empire Chapter 3) was a synthetic court religion two centuries after Majapahit's Shiva-Buddha and far less durable — a personal imperial cult that barely outlived its founder, where Majapahit's fusion had its own scripture and lasted on Bali for five centuries.

6.matchText: Mainland Angkor, the Khmer capital, had been a hydraulic metropolis
6.blurb: Khmer Empire Chapter 3 is the mainland's vastly larger version of the same idea. Angkor's barays and canals made it one of the largest pre-industrial settlement complexes ever identified, three centuries before Trowulan. Both are hydraulic capitals — rice, water, and a god-king — at opposite ends of the same Indianized engineering tradition, one riverine and huge, one maritime and smaller.
Hayam Wuruk's golden-age court, chronicled in the Nagarakretagama, is Java's Angkor moment — a Hindu-Buddhist sacred monarchy at its zenith.
Majapahit's syncretic Hindu-Buddhist statecraft is a Southeast Asian parallel to the religiously plural empires forming elsewhere in Asia.

7.matchText: the same problem this project flagged for Srivijaya
7.blurb: The single tightest cross-reference in the book. Srivijaya Chapter 4 works out, for the predecessor sea-empire, the identical question Majapahit poses: a thalassocracy is a network of sea lanes and tribute, not a map of administered provinces, and a flattering source inflates the network into an empire.

The Indian end of Majapahit's trade world. The Chola maritime empire's Tamil merchant guilds operated across the same Southeast Asian sea lane that fed Java's spice-and-rice entrepot. Medieval India Chapter 5 is the South Indian side of the network Majapahit sat in the middle of.
Majapahit's thalassocracy — tribute, trade, and contested 'how big was it really' — replays the Srivijaya question one civilization later.

9.matchText: enormous armadas of hundreds of ships and tens of thousands of men
9.blurb: Ming Chapter 3 is the treasure fleets from the Chinese side: the seven voyages, the giant ships, and why they stopped. The 1406 incident off Java — 170 of Zheng He's men killed in the Paregreg fighting and settled with a diplomatic bill, not an invasion — is a perfect miniature of where power sat in these waters.

9.matchText: the way it had become Indianized a thousand years earlier
9.blurb: The mechanism is identical to the one in Chapter 1: religion arriving through trade, prestige, and slow elite adoption from the coast inward, not by conquest. Khmer Chapter 1 is the template; Java's Islamization runs the same play a thousand years later with a different religion.
Zheng He's treasure fleets touched Majapahit's world just as Islam arrived — the twin external forces that reshaped maritime Southeast Asia.

10.matchText: the island Majapahit had conquered around 1343
10.blurb: Srivijaya Chapter 8 is the inheritance pattern from the other island: as Srivijaya dissolved into the Muslim world its Malay literary and political afterlife passed to Malacca, just as Majapahit's Hindu-Javanese world relocated, intact, to Bali. Two sea-empires, two very different afterlives.

5.matchText: the Vietnamese king Lê Thánh Tông's obliteration of the Cham capital Vijaya in 1471
5.blurb: The closest parallel in the chain. Đại Việt Chapter 8 tells the 1471 destruction of the Cham capital Vijaya the same way this book tells Bubat: a national hero's atrocity against a culturally distinct neighbour, remembered as glorious unification on the victor's side and as the end of a people on the other. Gajah Mada and Lê Thánh Tông are the same figure seen from two sides of a massacre.
